title.en: Workplace Sexual Harassment — Filing a POSH Complaint: ICC, Timelines and Protections

answerBox.en: Under the POSH Act, every workplace with 10+ employees must have an Internal Committee (IC). File your written complaint to the IC within 3 months of the incident (extendable by 3 more); the IC must complete its inquiry in 90 days, with recommendations acted on in 60 days. Interim reliefs — transfer, leave up to 3 months — are available during inquiry, and retaliation is itself punishable.

en.label: File with the IC
en.startsFrom: from the incident (IC can extend 3 more)

en.title: Write the complaint — specific, dated, evidenced
en.summary: Six copies to the IC with incidents, dates, witnesses.
en.detail: Address a written complaint to the **Internal Committee** (the POSH policy/notice board names members — an external member and woman presiding officer are mandatory) describing each incident with **dates, places, what was said/done, and witnesses**; attach messages, emails, call logs. The Act covers quid pro quo demands, hostile-environment conduct, and harassment by **anyone at the workplace** — colleagues, seniors, clients, vendors — including at offsites and work travel. If writing is difficult, IC members must assist.

en.title: Use the interim protections — they exist for you
en.summary: Transfer, leave, no-contact — during the inquiry.
en.detail: On request, the IC can recommend **interim measures**: transfer of you or the respondent, **leave up to 3 months** (beyond your entitlement), restraining the respondent from supervising/evaluating you, and no-contact directions. **Retaliation** — termination, demotion, hostile appraisals for complaining — violates the Act and service rules; document any and report it to the IC immediately as a fresh violation.

en.title: The inquiry: your procedural rights
en.summary: Natural justice, cross-examination via the IC, confidentiality.
en.detail: The IC follows service rules/natural justice: both sides heard, evidence and witnesses examined, questions to the other side routed through the IC (no direct confrontation), minimum 3 members present, and strict **confidentiality** (Section 16 bars publication of identities; breach is penalised). The IC report recommends action — from apology/warning to termination — plus **compensation** to you (deductible from the respondent's salary), assessed on trauma, career loss and medical costs. Appeals lie to the court/tribunal within **90 days**.

en.title: No IC? Small workplace? Criminal overlap?
en.summary: Local Committee, SHe-Box, and the parallel criminal route.
en.detail: Workplaces under 10 employees or complaints **against the employer himself** go to the district **Local Committee** (through the District Officer/DM). The central **[SHe-Box portal](https://shebox.wcd.gov.in)** routes complaints to the right IC/LC and tracks them; an employer with no IC faces penalties up to ₹50,000 and licence consequences. The criminal law runs in parallel when you choose: **Section 354A IPC (now Section 75 BNS)** for sexual harassment, with stalking/voyeurism provisions alongside — an FIR does not need the IC's permission, and the IC route does not bar it.

en.footnote: Malicious-complaint penalties in the Act require proven malice — inability to prove an allegation is expressly NOT malice (Section 14). Genuine complainants should not be deterred by that clause.

en.q: The incident was 5 months ago. Can I still complain?
en.a: Yes — the IC can extend the 3-month window by another 3 months for reasons recorded (fear, ongoing harassment, not knowing the process all qualify). Beyond 6 months, the Local Committee/courts have shown flexibility in continuing-harassment situations; and the criminal route has its own longer limitations. File with an explanation rather than not filing.

en.q: What if the harasser is the founder/MD — the IC reports to him?
en.a: Complaints against the employer go to the district Local Committee, not the IC — precisely for this conflict. SHe-Box routes it correctly. IC members' independence obligations and the external member requirement also exist for the senior-respondent problem; escalate to the LC if the IC is visibly captured.

Section 16 prohibits publishing or disclosing the identities of complainant, respondent and witnesses, and inquiry contents — with penalties for breach. Internally, information flows on need-to-know within the IC process. Leaks by colleagues/management are themselves violations to record and report.

The POSH Act's complainant is defined as an "aggrieved woman". Men facing workplace sexual harassment use service rules/HR policy (many companies extend gender-neutral internal policies), criminal law (assault/insult provisions in the BNS), and civil remedies. Company policy routes are worth checking first — most large employers now run gender-neutral processes.

en.q: I complained and was fired two weeks later. What are my options?
en.a: Retaliation timing is powerful evidence: report it to the IC/LC as victimisation, and challenge the termination (see our wrongful-termination guide) with the POSH complaint as the mala fide backdrop. Courts and labour forums treat post-complaint terminations with deep suspicion — preserve the timeline meticulously.

reviewer: Adv. Priya Sharma, Bar Council of Delhi, D/1284/2016
author: WakilBhai Editorial Team
